section.title: Joseph Sold
section.sectionRationale: The account of Jacob now narrows to Joseph, the favored son whose dreams of rule enrage his brothers. Envy hardens into a plot; Joseph is stripped, thrown into a pit, and sold into Egypt, while his father is left to mourn a son he believes is dead.
section.thesis: Human envy and betrayal do their worst against the beloved dreamer, yet the very act that seems to bury God's purpose becomes the hidden road by which He will save the family, and the world, foreshadowing a greater beloved Son.
section.frameNote: Four movements: the favored son and his dreams; the murderous plot at Dothan; Joseph sold to the traders; and Jacob's inconsolable mourning.

units.label: The favored son and his dreams
units.note: loved, hated, and dreaming of rule

verses.text: Now Jacob lived in the land where his father had resided, the land of Canaan.

This is the account of Jacob. When Joseph was seventeen years old, he was tending the flock with his brothers, the sons of his father’s wives Bilhah and Zilpah, and he brought their father a bad report about them.
Now Israel loved Joseph more than his other sons, because Joseph had been born to him in his old age; so he made him a {{ketonet|robe of many colors}}.
When Joseph’s brothers saw that their father loved him more than any of them, they {{sane|hated}} him and could not speak a kind word to him.
Then Joseph had a {{chalom|dream}}, and when he told it to his brothers, they hated him even more.
He said to them, “Listen to this dream I had:
We were binding sheaves of grain in the field, and suddenly my sheaf rose and stood upright, while your sheaves gathered around and bowed down to mine.”
“Do you intend to reign over us?” his brothers asked. “Will you actually rule us?” So they hated him even more because of his dream and his statements.
Then Joseph had another dream and told it to his brothers. “Look,” he said, “I had another dream, and this time the sun and moon and eleven stars were bowing down to me.”
He told his father and brothers, but his father rebuked him and said, “What is this dream that you have had? Will your mother and brothers and I actually come and bow down to the ground before you?”
And his brothers were jealous of him, but his father kept in mind what he had said.
Some time later, Joseph’s brothers had gone to pasture their father’s flocks near Shechem.
Israel said to him, “Are not your brothers pasturing the flocks at Shechem? Get ready; I am sending you to them.” “I am ready,” Joseph replied.
Then Israel told him, “Go now and see how your brothers and the flocks are faring, and bring word back to me.” So he sent him off from the Valley of Hebron. And when Joseph arrived in Shechem,
a man found him wandering in the field and asked, “What are you looking for?”
“I am looking for my brothers,” Joseph replied. “Can you please tell me where they are pasturing their flocks?”
“They have moved on from here,” the man answered. “I heard them say, ‘Let us go to Dothan.’” So Joseph set out after his brothers and found them at Dothan.
Now Joseph’s brothers saw him in the distance, and before he arrived, they plotted to kill him.
“Here comes that dreamer!” they said to one another.
“Come now, let us kill him and throw him into one of the pits. We can say that a vicious animal has devoured him. Then we shall see what becomes of his dreams!”
When Reuben heard this, he tried to rescue Joseph from their hands. “Let us not take his life,” he said.
“Do not shed his blood. Throw him into this pit in the wilderness, but do not lay a hand on him.” Reuben said this so that he could rescue Joseph from their hands and return him to his father.
So when Joseph came to his brothers, they stripped him of his robe, the robe of many colors he was wearing,
and they took him and threw him into the pit. Now the pit was empty, with no water in it.
And as they sat down to eat a meal, they looked up and saw a caravan of Ishmaelites coming from Gilead. Their camels were carrying spices, balm, and myrrh on their way down to Egypt.
Then Judah said to his brothers, “What profit will we gain if we kill our brother and cover up his blood?
Come, let us sell him to the Ishmaelites and not lay a hand on him; for he is our brother, our own flesh.” And they agreed.
So when the Midianite traders passed by, his brothers pulled Joseph out of the pit and sold him for twenty shekels of silver to the Ishmaelites, who took him to Egypt.
When Reuben returned to the pit and saw that Joseph was not there, he tore his clothes,
returned to his brothers, and said, “The boy is gone! What am I going to do?”
Then they took Joseph’s robe, slaughtered a young goat, and dipped the robe in its blood.
They sent the robe of many colors to their father and said, “We found this. Examine it to see whether it is your son’s robe or not.”
His father recognized it and said, “It is my son’s robe! A vicious animal has devoured him. Joseph has surely been torn to pieces!”
Then Jacob tore his clothes, put sackcloth around his waist, and mourned for his son many days.
All his sons and daughters tried to comfort him, but he refused to be comforted. “No,” he said. “I will go down to {{sheol|Sheol}} mourning for my son.” So his father wept for him.
Meanwhile, the Midianites sold Joseph in Egypt to Potiphar, an officer of Pharaoh and captain of the guard.

ketonet.gloss: A special tunic, possibly long-sleeved or richly ornamented, the robe of many colors. It marks Joseph as his father's favorite and, some suggest, as the one set over his brothers. The brothers strip it from him and dip it in blood, so the badge of love becomes the token of his supposed death.

sane.gloss: To hate. The word is repeated as the brothers' hatred deepens with each of Joseph's words and dreams, verses 4, 5, and 8. Their hatred, born of envy at their father's favor, is the engine that drives them toward betrayal, echoing the hatred that would later meet the beloved Son.

chalom.gloss: A dream. Dreams frame the whole Joseph story, from these boyhood visions of sheaves and stars to Pharaoh's dreams in Egypt. Here they reveal God's purpose that Joseph will one day rule and be bowed to, a purpose the brothers try in vain to kill.

sheol.gloss: The grave, the realm of the dead. Jacob says he will go down to Sheol still mourning, a picture of grief that expects no comfort this side of death. It heightens the darkness of the moment, for the reader knows the son Jacob mourns is alive and being carried toward God's purpose.

basic.context: The story now follows Joseph, Jacob's favorite son. His special robe and his dreams of his family bowing to him stir his brothers' hatred. When he is sent to check on them, they seize him, throw him into a pit, and sell him to traders bound for Egypt. They deceive Jacob into thinking Joseph is dead.
basic.hermeneutics: The key question is where God is in all this evil. He is never mentioned in the chapter, yet the whole story turns on His hidden hand. What the brothers mean for harm, God is quietly bending toward salvation, as later chapters will reveal.
basic.originalLanguages: The robe of many colors marks Joseph as favored. The word hated is repeated as the brothers' envy grows. Dreams, which frame the whole Joseph story, reveal God's purpose that Joseph will rule. And Jacob speaks of going down to Sheol, the grave, in unending grief for a son who is in fact alive.
basic.typology: Joseph is a striking picture of Christ: the beloved son, sent by his father to his brothers, rejected and stripped, sold for silver, and given up for dead, yet destined to be exalted and to save. Judah's role in the sale even echoes the later betrayal for silver.
basic.christ: Like Joseph, Jesus is the Father's beloved Son, sent to His own who hated Him, betrayed for pieces of silver, stripped of His garment, and handed over to seeming death. And like Joseph, He is raised to rule and to save the very ones who wronged Him.
basic.god: God is unseen but sovereign. Through envy, betrayal, and a slave sale, He is beginning to work the rescue of Jacob's family from famine. The chapter teaches that God's purposes advance even through, and especially through, the sins of others.
basic.crossReferences: Psalm 105 says God sent Joseph ahead as a slave to preserve life. Genesis 50 states plainly that what the brothers meant for evil, God meant for good. Acts 7 and the Gospels echo the pattern of a rejected deliverer.
basic.oneStory: This is the beginning of the road that carries Israel down into Egypt. A beloved son is betrayed and sold, seemingly the end of God's promise, but it is the hidden way God will preserve the covenant family and point ahead to the beloved Son who saves through rejection.

context.literary: This opens the toledot of Jacob, the last and longest narrative cycle in Genesis, dominated by Joseph. Repeated motifs, the robe, the dreams, the deceiving of a father with a slaughtered goat, echo earlier scenes: Jacob himself once deceived his father with goatskins, and now he is deceived with a goat's blood.
context.historical: Dothan lay on a trade route north of Shechem. Caravans of Ishmaelite and Midianite traders carried spices, balm, and myrrh down to Egypt. Twenty shekels of silver was a standard price for a young male slave in the period, grounding the sale in real economic custom.
context.canonical: The chapter launches a narrative that Scripture repeatedly interprets: Psalm 105:16-22 and Genesis 45 and 50 read Joseph's suffering as God's providential means of preservation. The pattern of the rejected-then-exalted deliverer becomes a template the New Testament applies to Christ.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: Moses shows how God preserves the covenant family through the sinful actions of Joseph's brothers, teaching Israel to trust God's hidden providence and setting up the great deliverance from famine that keeps the promise alive.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes favoritism, envy, and betrayal without endorsing any of them. Jacob's partiality and the brothers' hatred are portrayed as destructive. The enduring truth is God's sovereign providence overruling human sin for good.

debates.question: Who actually sold Joseph, the Ishmaelites or the Midianites?

views.label: One caravan, two names
views.case: The terms Ishmaelite and Midianite are used interchangeably for the same traveling merchants, as elsewhere in Scripture, so the brothers sold Joseph to a single caravan described by both names.

views.label: A layered account
views.case: Some read a deliberately compressed telling in which Midianites drew Joseph up and the Ishmaelites carried him to Egypt, and critical scholars posit combined sources; even so, the canonical text presents one coherent event.

debates.bearing: Either way the outcome is the same: Joseph's brothers are responsible for handing him over, and he is carried to Egypt, where God will place him to save many lives.

study.originalLanguages: The ketonet passim, the ornate robe, singles Joseph out as beloved, and its stripping and bloodying turns favor into the sign of supposed death. The verb sane, hated, drums through the opening scene, intensifying with every dream and word until hatred hardens into a plot. Those dreams, chalom, carry God's purpose that Joseph will rule, a purpose the brothers cannot kill. And Jacob's vow to go down to Sheol mourning deepens the darkness, since the reader knows the mourned son is alive in God's hands.

typology.type: Joseph the beloved son, sent by his father to his brothers
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the Father's beloved Son, sent to His own people who received Him not.

typology.type: Joseph rejected, stripped, and sold for silver
typology.fulfillment: Jesus betrayed for silver, stripped of His garments, and handed over to death.

typology.type: Joseph given up for dead yet preserved to save
typology.fulfillment: The crucified and risen Christ, exalted to rule and to save those who wronged Him.

study.christ: Joseph's story opens as a portrait of Christ. He is the father's specially beloved son, sent from the valley to seek his brothers, who hate him for his words and his coming reign. They strip his robe, cast him into a pit, and sell him for pieces of silver, an echo the Gospels will sound again when Judah's namesake betrays Jesus. Joseph descends, seemingly to death, yet is carried into Egypt on the road to exaltation. So Christ, rejected and killed, is raised to reign and to rescue the very brothers who handed Him over, turning their worst act into the world's salvation.
study.god: God is not named once in this chapter, yet He is its true actor. The unnamed man who redirects Joseph to Dothan, the timely caravan, the restraining of Reuben and Judah from murder, all move Joseph precisely toward Egypt. What the brothers intend as the death of a dream, God is using as the first step of a rescue. Here is the mystery of providence: God governs even human sin, never approving it, yet weaving it into His good and saving purpose.

crossReferences.note: Joseph's own summary: you intended to harm me, but God intended it for good, to save many lives.
God sent Joseph ahead as a slave to preserve life through the coming famine.
Stephen recounts that the patriarchs sold Joseph, but God was with him and rescued him.
Judas betrays Jesus for silver, echoing the sale of Joseph by his brothers.

study.oneStory: This chapter starts the descent that will bring Israel into Egypt and, centuries later, out again in the Exodus. A beloved son is betrayed and sold, and the promise seems buried in a pit. But God is quietly turning betrayal into the road of rescue, preserving the covenant family and sketching in advance the pattern of the beloved Son who saves the world by being rejected, sold, and raised.

quiz.q: What stirred the brothers' hatred of Joseph to the breaking point?

options.text: Their father's favoritism and Joseph's dreams of them bowing to him.
options.note: The robe and the dreams of rule fueled envy into hatred, verses 3 to 11.

options.text: Joseph's cruelty toward them in the fields.
options.note: Joseph brought a bad report, but the text centers the cause on favor and the dreams, not cruelty.

options.text: A dispute over inheritance money.
options.note: Money is not the issue; the envy is over their father's love and Joseph's dreams.

options.text: Joseph refusing to tend the flocks.
options.note: Joseph was tending the flocks and was sent to check on his brothers.

quiz.q: Although God is never named in the chapter, how is He at work?

options.text: He providentially moves Joseph toward Egypt to preserve the family from famine.
options.note: Later Scripture reveals God's hidden hand, Genesis 50:20 and Psalm 105.

options.text: He is absent, having abandoned the family to their sin.
options.note: God's silence is not absence; He is sovereignly guiding events.

options.text: He punishes Joseph for his pride by ending his story.
options.note: Joseph's story is only beginning; God is preserving, not punishing him.

options.text: He miraculously rescues Joseph from the pit at once.
options.note: Joseph is sold into slavery; God's rescue unfolds over years, not instantly.

quiz.q: How is Joseph in this chapter a picture of Christ?

options.text: A beloved son sent to his brothers, rejected, stripped, and sold for silver.
options.note: The pattern mirrors Christ's rejection and betrayal for silver.

options.text: A powerful king who conquers his enemies by force.
options.note: That comes later in a different key; here the likeness is in rejection and suffering.

options.text: A priest offering sacrifices at an altar.
options.note: Joseph is not pictured as a priest here; the parallel is the rejected beloved son.

options.text: A prophet who calls the nations to repent.
options.note: The Christ-parallel in this chapter is suffering and betrayal, not prophetic preaching.

quiz.q: What did the brothers do to deceive their father?

options.text: They dipped Joseph's robe in goat's blood and let Jacob assume he was dead.
options.note: The badge of favor became the token of supposed death, verses 31 to 33.

options.text: They told him plainly that they had sold Joseph.
options.note: They hid the truth; Jacob learns it only years later.

options.text: They claimed Joseph had run away to Egypt.
options.note: They implied a wild animal killed him, not that he fled.

options.text: They said Joseph drowned in a well.
options.note: They said a vicious animal devoured him.

quiz.q: How does this chapter fit the larger biblical story?

options.text: It begins the road into Egypt by which God preserves the covenant family.
options.note: The betrayal is the hidden means of rescue and the setup for the Exodus.

options.text: It marks the failure and end of God's promise to Abraham.
options.note: The promise seems threatened but is in fact being preserved by God.

options.text: It shows that dreams are meaningless distractions.
options.note: The dreams carry God's true purpose and come to pass.

options.text: It concludes the story of Joseph.
options.note: It only begins Joseph's story, which fills the rest of Genesis.

questions: Jacob's favoritism sowed years of pain in his family. Where might partiality, even unintended, be doing quiet damage in your relationships?
The brothers hated Joseph for his dreams and his father's love. How do you handle the envy that rises when someone else seems favored or gifted?
God was working through betrayal that no one could see as good at the time. How does Joseph's story help you trust God in seasons when His hand is hidden?
Joseph foreshadows the beloved Son who saves through rejection. How does seeing Christ in this story deepen your worship of the One who was sold and stripped for you?
Jacob refused to be comforted, certain his son was dead, while Joseph was alive in God's care. Where do you need to hold space for hope in a grief that feels final?

translationNotes.note: Robe of many colors renders a Hebrew phrase of uncertain meaning; it may indicate a long-sleeved or richly ornamented tunic. Either way it marks Joseph as specially favored.
Ishmaelites and Midianites appear to describe the same caravan of traders, used interchangeably here as elsewhere in Scripture; twenty shekels was a customary price for a young slave.
Sheol denotes the grave or realm of the dead, not a place of torment; Jacob means he will grieve until he himself dies.
